Author: Timo Laitila
Title: Using Claude Code for organizing your second brain
Text:
Using Claude Code for organizing your second brain
I spent this weekend familiarizing myself with one of the most hyped AI-assisted software development tools at the moment: Claude Code.
Claude Code
Claude Code, as the name implies, is the popular coding assistant from Anthropic, known for its advanced LLM models such as Claude Sonnet and Claude Opus. These models are well suited for planning, designing, and — first and foremost — generating software code.
Claude Sonnet has recently become the go-to option for coders and is arguably still the best medium-priced model, even now that GPT-5 is out. The race is on, and things may look very different in the coming weeks and months.
To quickly describe the main benefit of Claude Code: it’s a terminal-based tool. Compared to other approaches like Copilot integrated into your IDE or ChatGPT in a web browser, Claude Code has a clear edge by being able to access the local file system in the folder where it’s launched. Naturally, it’s up to the user to decide how much autonomy they allow Claude Code, but in theory, the tool can do basically everything a human user can via the terminal.
This approach isn’t unique in the sense that many serious contenders are now entering the market with similar tools — such as OpenAI Codex, Google’s Gemini CLI, and Warp, which takes things further by fully integrating the terminal with AI.
Obsidian
As I wanted to try out Claude Code’s capabilities with something a bit more exotic, I stumbled upon a use case that sounded interesting and, more importantly, very topical for me. I also found it appealing because the example demonstrates how to expand LLM tools beyond their original problem domain into something totally new.
The problem I wanted to explore with Claude Code relates to my recent pursuit of taking my note-taking skills to the next level. A few months ago, I made a shift from a personal Dokuwiki-based private wiki to a tool called Obsidian. I had known about Obsidian for a while, but only recently was I ready to give it a try — and I quickly fell in love with its power and capabilities. So much so that I migrated my wiki to Obsidian almost immediately and haven’t regretted the choice. Obsidian is really like an IDE for your notes. It feels modern and is super customizable, so it fits every imaginable use case.
The main reason I love Obsidian, however, is that it gives me full ownership of all the content I create. This is similar to Dokuwiki, but Obsidian makes it even more lightweight and fluent. The fact that everything centers around markdown files makes it feel so natural. With Obsidian, you can always have peace of mind knowing your notes are safe (you can use cloud folders or Git if you want full version control) and accessible by any system or tool that supports markdown or basic text editing. So even if Obsidian were to vanish someday, my content wouldn’t be affected in any way.
There are lots of good introduction videos on YouTube about Obsidian if you want to learn more.
Zettelkasten and second brain
While Obsidian is first and foremost a tool, its full power lies in supporting the organization of your thoughts and notes — what is often referred to as the “second brain.” There are as many implementations as there are users, but many are based on a technique called Zettelkasten.
The simplified idea of Zettelkasten is that all thoughts are divided into self-contained, atomic cards that are interconnected with links and tags. The notes and thoughts thus form a network resembling how the human brain works.
One main best practice of using the Zettelkasten method is keeping traditional folders to a minimum. Folders are a tempting and natural way to organize work-related data (or any other data). Having folders like HR, Projects, Products, Finance, etc., comes very naturally without much thought.
However, there’s a major drawback with folders: the structure soon becomes overly complicated as your understanding grows and new connections are created. Even if there was a solid idea in the beginning, during hectic periods the idea quickly gets lost, and new folders are created in parallel with — and inside — the existing ones. Soon there’s no visible logic, and adding new files to folders becomes a guessing game.
This was exactly the situation I was in with my previous note-taking system. There were plenty of notes and documents organized by theme, but I wasn’t happy with the structure. Moreover, finding relevant stuff in the complicated hierarchy was nearly impossible. I often had the feeling that I had a certain piece of knowledge, idea, or thought somewhere, but finding it — even with search tools — was difficult, seriously hindering the building of my personal knowledge.
This is where Claude Code jumps in.
Obsidian, Claude Code and second brain in action
After merging my notes from Dokuwiki to Obsidian, I preserved the structure of the notes in their theme-based folders. I had already started moving towards Zettelkasten, and all my new documents were placed in a flat folder (creatively named “Documents”) with relevant tags and links.
One unexpected benefit of using Obsidian with its markdown based systems for note-taking is that, since it’s all just text files, it naturally plays well with any LLM tool. This wasn’t something I had in my mind when I chose Obsidian, but once that realization hit — and I get Claude Code in my hands — it was like witnessing a match made in heaven unfold right before my eyes.
Now I wanted to give Claude Code a chance to impress me by organizing my legacy notes from theme-based folders into one “Documents” folder and adding links and tags along the way. I also wanted Claude Code to create relevant Map of Content (MoC) files to summarize each topic and generate link lists. These MoC documents would then replace the old rigid folders, offering added flexibility to handpick topics and cross-link freely — without artificial folder structures restricting the thinking.
As I started this experiment, I of course made a copy of my Obsidian folder and had Claude Code work against this isolated sandbox to avoid risking my precious note-taking history spanning multiple years.
Phase 1. Getting started
Once I had the sandbox ready, I launched Claude Code in the terminal and first asked it to analyze its environment. It quickly identified that it was in an Obsidian folder (“Vault” in Obsidian terminology) and summarized its findings. I then asked Claude Code to create a summary file based on its findings (this is easy with the `/init` command), and it soon created one. Claude Code correctly identified and interpreted my key Zettelkasten folders as well as my theme-specific folders and the variety of notes I had in them.
Phase 2. Tasklist
Before rushing into implementation, I told Claude Code what I wanted it to accomplish (move notes from theme folders, add metadata, and create MoC documents) and asked it to first create a step-by-step task list for the process.
Claude Code soon created a detailed task list that looked solid and actionable, giving me the ability to follow and control what it would do with my data. The main steps of the plan were:
- Analysis & Planning. Analyze existing files, identify themes and topics, design tags and topics, plan MoCs
- Preparation. Plan what metadata to add to files and how to link them
- Implementation. Add metadata and tags, move files, create links
- Finalization. Create MoC files, check for link integrity
Phase 3. Analysis & Planning
With the solid step-by-step plan in place, I allowed Claude to begin the first actual step: Analysis & Planning.
Claude Code soon evaluated my Obsidian vault and summarized a total of 219 markdown files, of which over 90 were in theme-specific folders. Given this is my isolated work-related vault, the size is not overly huge and reflects the fact that my legacy notes are often large documents rather than atomic ‘ideas’ as Zettelkasten proposes.
Based on its scan, Claude Code identified the following themes, which I approved:
- People & Organization
- Projects & Development
- Processes & Standards
- Management & Leadership
- Technology & Tools
It also proposed a multitude of content type, subject area, and organization-specific tags, which I approved for now.
Next, Claude Code suggested some key MoC documents summarizing the above themes, as well as a template structure for MoC documents — which looked fine, even though I hadn’t asked for it.
Overall, this planning phase took roughly 30 minutes while Claude Code browsed through the various folders in the file system. Much of that time went into me granting access rights for various command-line operations (Claude Code asks for permission for each command, and you can either give one-time or permanent access). Overall, the operation was smooth and the results impressive.
Phase 4. Implementation & Finalization
With no open questions left from the planning phase, it was finally time to jump into implementation.
After I gave Claude Code permission to proceed, it first tested adding metadata to four initial documents. As everything looked good, Claude continued adding metadata to all remaining files.
Once the metadata was in place, the next step was to move the files into the “Documents” folder. Finally, Claude Code created the planned MoC files, placed them in a separate directory as agreed, and provided a summary of the completed migration (a total of 95 files migrated, relevant tags added, and 9 MoC files created).
The implementation step took less time than I anticipated. The total time from starting the analysis to finalizing implementation was roughly one hour. Again, much of the time was spent granting access, as I wanted to stay fully in control of what Claude was doing.
Conclusions
After the bulk of the migration was done, I browsed through the notes and was very happy with the results. The MoC files obviously weren’t perfect, but they provided a solid foundation to build on. (As a general note, it’s not recommended to let AI tools create content in your personal vault, since that goes against the principle of you — very personally — owning every word it contains.)
I was actually so happy with the migration and the visibility the MoC files created that I wanted to go further and let Claude fix some other topics I had intentionally left out from the first phase. These included improving how I manage tasks and daily notes, and expanding the migration to cover attachments. Now, after these changes, my Obsidian vault looks pretty clean and solid — and above all, I finally know precisely where everything is located!
The only drawback I can currently think of is that, while I let Claude Code propose tags for documents quite freely, I now have a great variety of them — including some duplicate and many that appear only once. This isn’t a huge deal, since Obsidian is very flexible and powerful with tags, but maybe a bit less would bring more clarity. Now that I think of it, rationalizing tags might be the next task I give Claude Code — once I’ve had enough time to familiarize myself with the new structure and play around with it to validate whether that’s really what I need.
Based on my experience, I can warmly recommend hooking AI tools into your note-taking system — especially if you’re using Obsidian or something similar that makes it possible. Just be sure to understand how you want to apply AI capabilities, and use them as helpers rather than letting them own or mess with your content. The whole exercise took me few hours total calendar time, but I was occasionally free for other activities while Claude was working on my documents. Honestly, the workload was so big that I probably would’ve never even started manually!
Finally, looking ahead, one interesting and promising step would be to explore the agentic capabilities of AI tools — essentially giving them more autonomy — and look for use cases where they could offer continuous support and clarity to your system. Maybe that’s something I’ll dive into next, once I’ve settled into my new, fancy Obsidian setup and stopped admiring how organized everything suddenly looks.
